description: Calculate power factor (cos φ) and corrective capacitor size. Determine P, S, Q, and kVAR compensation for AC circuits.
intro: Basic electronics is governed by Ohm's Law, Kirchhoff's Laws, and semiconductor principles. Calculating resistance, power, voltage, and current forms the foundation for designing and maintaining circuits. Learn how to calculate power factor cos(φ) and size the capacitor needed to correct it. Determine P (W), S (VA), Q (VAR), and kVAR compensation values. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you up to date.
keyTakeaway: **cos(φ) = P/S**. Target > 0.95. Corrective capacitor: Qc = P × (tan(φ₁) − tan(φ₂)). Avoids utility penalties.
useCases: Circuit design and analysis involving power factor and capacitive correction
Validate theoretical calculations before building circuits
Estimate parameters for microcontroller projects (Arduino, ESP32, STM32)

example.result: A 45 µF capacitor improves power factor from 0.7 to 0.95

explanation:
**Electronics** is governed by Ohm's Law, Kirchhoff's Laws, and Faraday's principles. Applying the correct formulas prevents costly errors and electrical failures.

## How Power Factor Works

Power factor (cos φ) measures how effectively your AC circuit uses electrical power. It's the ratio of real power (watts) to apparent power (volt-amperes). Most utilities penalize commercial or industrial users when power factor drops below 0.95, leading to higher bills. This calculator helps you determine the reactive power compensation (in kVAR) and the size of the capacitor needed to correct low power factors.

The calculator applies the formula with your input values, checking against typical ranges and alerting if values fall outside normal parameters.

## Important Notes

This calculation is a reference estimate. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult with a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.a: Power factor is the ratio of real power to apparent power in an AC circuit, expressed as cos(φ). It ranges from 0 to 1, with 1.0 being ideal. Most utilities prefer > 0.95.

faq.q: Why do utilities penalize low power factor?
faq.a: Low power factor means your circuit draws more current to deliver the same real power, increasing transmission losses and stress on the grid. Commercial and industrial users typically face surcharges when cos(φ) < 0.95.

faq.q: Does power factor matter for residential use?
faq.a: Most residential customers don't face penalties, but improving power factor reduces cable losses. Power factor correction is rarely installed in homes.

faq.q: Is the corrective capacitor always a single unit?
faq.a: For fixed loads, yes. For variable loads, a capacitor bank with automatic contactors is used to adjust compensation dynamically.

faq.q: Can I use capacitors to correct DC circuits?
faq.a: No — power factor (cos φ) applies only to AC circuits. DC circuits have no reactive component.

faq.q: What happens if I overcorrect the power factor?
faq.a: Over-correction makes the circuit capacitive, causing problems similar to low inductive power factor but in the opposite direction. Aim for 0.95–0.98, not 1.0 exactly.

faq.q: Is this calculator free to use?
faq.a: Yes, all our calculators are free and require no registration.
Results are estimates using standard formulas validated internationally. For critical applications, consult a licensed electrician or engineer.
No. All calculations run in your browser. We don't send any data to our servers.
